title: A Loan Plan for Buying Houses Helped Rich People More
summary: In England, there was a plan called Help to Buy that started in 2013 to help people buy their first home. It gave loans for house deposits so people could get mortgages more easily. A group called the Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS) studied it and found that it mostly helped people who already earned a lot of money, especially in places where houses cost less. The plan did not make houses cheaper for many people because it could only be used for new houses, which are not common in most areas. It also had little effect on helping poor people move up in life. Some people say it made house prices go up, while others say it helped people buy homes and build more houses. The loan part for new houses is now closed in England and Scotland, but another part that helps with mortgages is still available across the UK.

background_read: In many countries, governments create plans to help people buy homes, especially first-time buyers who might not have enough money. These plans can include loans or guarantees to make it easier to get a mortgage. However, sometimes these plans don't work as intended and might help richer people more than poorer ones. Understanding how such plans affect house prices and who benefits is important for making fair rules.
Article_Structure: WHO: The Institute for Fiscal Studies (IFS) and the government in England
WHAT: A study showing that the Help to Buy loan plan mostly helped higher earners and had limited impact on making houses affordable
WHERE: England, with mentions of Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland
WHY: To report on how a government housing plan worked and who it benefited, as there are calls to bring it back

perspectives.description: Say Help to Buy pushed up house prices by letting people spend more
Say Help to Buy helped many people buy homes and boosted housebuilding
